Literature Digest

Apr 02 – Apr 16, 2026  ·  Chuong Lab, CU Boulder
1377 scanned
552 preprints
20/20 journals
keyword + AI

AI Summary

This digest delivers the strongest TE content in recent memory, led by three independently significant results. Nishihara & Komiya (Genome Biology) demonstrate that TEs drove mammalian-specific expansion of Sox2- and Brn2-binding regulatory modules in neural cells — a systems-level validation of TE exaptation as a regulatory rewiring engine. Zeng et al. (Cell) show cell-type-specific transposon demethylation during brain aging drives TAD disruption and transcriptional dysregulation, extending the TE reactivation framework from cancer to neurodegeneration. A preprint from Béliveau et al. (PanTEon) presents a cross-kingdom TE classification framework addressing a major reproducibility bottleneck in comparative TE analysis, while a separate preprint identifies a TE insertion disrupting a thyroglobulin-like receptor as the genetic basis of Bt toxin (Vip3Aa) resistance in the cotton bollworm — a striking example of TE-mediated adaptive evolution at an immune-related gene. Zumalave et al. (Science) use long-read sequencing of hypermobile tumors to reveal concurrent L1 insertions at two loci generate reciprocal chromosomal translocations, establishing a new class of TE-mediated genomic instability in cancer.

Innate immunity and RNA biology round out the highlights. Two papers reveal new metabolic checkpoints on IFN signaling: Qin et al. (PNAS) show SCD1-mediated membrane fatty acid desaturation licenses STING palmitoylation and IFN-beta production, and a preprint reports the COX2-PGE2-PKA axis suppresses antiviral STING activation by blocking mtDNA release — both relevant to how cancer cells might evade TE-driven innate immune signaling. Zuo et al. (Cell) discover pyruvylation, a new STAT1 post-translational modification induced by high glucose that suppresses IFN signaling. On RNA and epitranscriptomics, a preprint demonstrates m6A placement controls mRNA stability in a cell-type-specific manner in planarians, directly intersecting with the lab’s ongoing m6A/TE work, while Grillo et al. (Science Advances) show targeted long-read RNA-seq enables splicing variant diagnosis in rare diseases — a platform applicable to TE-derived isoform discovery.

Curated Highlights 30 papers · 6 topics
